1.1: After Moses the servant of the LORD died, the LORD spoke to Joshua son of Nun — Moses' faithful attendant — and said:
1.2: My servant Moses has died. Now rise — take all this people across the Jordan into the land I am giving them.
1.3: Every place your foot touches, I have already given to you — just as I declared to Moses.
1.4: Your domain stretches from the southern wilderness and Mount Lebanon northward to the great Euphrates River — the entire Hittite country — all the way west to the Mediterranean.
1.5: No one will ever be able to stand against you all your life. As I was present with Moses, I will be present with you — I will never abandon you or give up on you.
1.6: Be strong and full of courage — it is you who will lead this people into possession of the land the LORD swore to their ancestors.
1.7: Above all, be strong and deeply courageous — careful to live by every word of the Torah my servant Moses gave you. Do not veer from it to the right or the left, so that you may thrive in everything you do.
1.8: Let this Torah never leave your lips — murmur it, turn it over in your mind, day and night, and live by every word written there. That is the path to true prosperity; that is how you will succeed.
1.9: I have commanded you: be strong and bold. Do not be afraid; do not lose heart — the LORD your God goes with you every step of the way.
1.10: Joshua then gave orders to the commanders of the people:
1.11: "Move through the camp and say to the people: 'Prepare your provisions — in three days you cross this Jordan and enter the land the LORD your God is giving you to make your own.'"
1.12: Then Joshua addressed the Reubenites, Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh:
1.13: "Keep in mind what Moses the LORD's servant commanded you: 'The LORD your God is granting you rest and giving you this land.'"
1.14: "Your wives, children, and flocks may stay in the land Moses granted you east of the Jordan. But every warrior among you must cross over fully armed ahead of your brothers and fight alongside them"
1.15: "until the LORD settles your brothers into rest just as he has settled you, and they too take hold of the land the LORD your God is giving them. Then you may return to your territory east of the Jordan — the land Moses the LORD's servant gave you."
1.16: They answered Joshua: 'Every order you give us, we will carry out; wherever you send us, we will go.'
1.17: We obeyed Moses in everything, and we will obey you the same way. May the LORD your God be with you as he was with Moses!
1.18: Any man who defies your command and refuses to follow your word will be put to death. Be strong — be courageous.

2.1: Joshua son of Nun secretly dispatched two spies from Shittim with these orders: 'Scout the land — Jericho especially.' They went in and came to the house of a woman named Rahab, a prostitute, and spent the night there.
2.2: Word reached the king of Jericho: 'Israelites have arrived here tonight to scout our country.'
2.3: The king of Jericho sent word to Rahab: 'Hand over the men who came to your house — they have come to spy out this whole country.'
2.4: But the woman had already hidden the two men. She told the messengers: 'The men did come to me — but I had no idea where they were from.'
2.5: 'At dusk, just as the gate was closing, they left. I have no idea which way they went. Go after them now — you may still catch them!'
2.6: In fact, she had already taken them up to the roof and concealed them beneath the stalks of flax she had spread out there to dry.
2.7: The search party headed off along the road toward the Jordan as far as the river crossings. The gate was shut behind them the moment they left.
2.8: Before they settled in for the night, Rahab came up to them on the roof
2.9: and said: 'I know the LORD has given you this land. Your coming has thrown us all into dread — everyone in the country has dissolved in fear before you.'
2.10: 'We heard how the LORD dried up the Red Sea ahead of you when you left Egypt, and what you did to Sihon and Og, the two Amorite kings beyond the Jordan — utterly destroying them.'
2.11: 'The moment we heard it, our hearts collapsed. No one had any resolve left — for the LORD your God is God above in the heavens and below on the earth. There is no other.'
2.12: 'So now, swear to me by the LORD — since I have shown you loyal love, show that same steadfast loyalty to my family. Give me a reliable pledge'
2.13: 'that you will spare my father, my mother, my brothers, my sisters, and all who are theirs — that you will rescue every one of us from death.'
2.14: The men said to her: 'With our own lives we guarantee yours — we will die before you come to harm. Keep our mission secret, and when the LORD gives us this land, we will treat you with faithful, loyal love.'
2.15: She lowered them by a rope through her window — her house was built into the city wall, and she lived within the wall itself.
2.16: 'Head for the hills,' she told them. 'The search party must not find you. Stay hidden three days until they give up and come back — then you can be on your way.'
2.17: 'We will be clear of any guilt under this oath you've made us swear,' the men said,
2.18: 'only under these conditions: when we come in, tie this scarlet cord in the window you lowered us from, and bring your father, mother, brothers, and every member of your family inside with you.'
2.19: 'Anyone who steps outside your door into the street — their death is their own responsibility; we are not to blame. But anyone under your roof who is harmed — that blood is on us.'
2.20: 'If you betray our mission to anyone, we are released from this oath entirely.'
2.21: 'Agreed,' she said. 'Let it be exactly as you say.' She sent them off and they went, and she tied the scarlet cord in the window.
2.22: They departed into the hills and stayed three days until the search party came back. The pursuers had combed the whole route and found nothing.
2.23: The two men came back down from the hills, crossed the Jordan, and returned to Joshua son of Nun. They told him everything that had happened.
2.24: They told Joshua: 'The LORD has truly given the whole land into our hands — every single inhabitant there has melted in fear before us.'

3.1: Early in the morning Joshua led all Israel from Shittim to the banks of the Jordan, and they camped there before making the crossing.
3.2: Three days later the commanders moved through the camp
3.3: with this command for the people: 'When you see the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God being carried by the Levitical priests, break camp and follow it.'
3.4: 'Keep a gap of roughly two thousand cubits between you and the ark — do not close that distance — so you can see the route, for this is a road you have never walked before.'
3.5: Joshua said to the people: 'Set yourselves apart — consecrate yourselves — for tomorrow the LORD will do something extraordinary in your midst.'
3.6: Then Joshua said to the priests: 'Lift the ark of the covenant and lead the way across.' The priests took up the ark and moved to the front of the people.
3.7: The LORD said to Joshua: 'This day I begin to make your name great before all Israel, so that they know — as surely as I was with Moses, I am with you.'
3.8: Give this charge to the priests who carry the ark of the covenant: 'When your feet touch the edge of the Jordan's water, stand still right there in the river.'
3.9: Joshua called to all Israel: 'Come near — listen to the words of the LORD your God.'
3.10: 'By what happens today you will know that the living God is in your midst — and that he will drive out from before you the Canaanites, Hittites, Hivites, Perizzites, Girgashites, Amorites, and Jebusites without fail.'
3.11: Look — the ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth leads the way into the Jordan.
3.12: Select twelve men from the tribes of Israel — one from each tribe.
3.13: The moment the feet of the priests carrying the ark of the LORD — Lord of all the earth — touch the Jordan's water, the flow will be cut off. The water coming down from upstream will pile up in a single heap.
3.14: When the people set out from their tents to cross the Jordan, the priests carried the ark of the covenant out in front of them.
3.15: As the priests who carried the ark came to the Jordan and their feet dipped into the edge of the water — the Jordan was at full flood, overflowing all its banks throughout the harvest —
3.16: the water flowing downstream was cut off entirely and rose in a great heap far upstream at the town of Adam, near Zarethan. The water flowing toward the Dead Sea drained away completely. The people crossed on dry ground directly opposite Jericho.
3.17: The priests carrying the ark of the covenant stood firm on dry ground in the middle of the Jordan riverbed while all Israel crossed over — every last person — until the whole nation had gone across on dry ground.

4.1: Once every person had crossed the Jordan, the LORD said to Joshua:
4.2: "Select twelve men from among the people — one from each tribe —"
4.3: "and give them this charge: 'Lift out twelve stones from the Jordan riverbed — from the exact place where the priests stood firm — carry them with you, and set them down at tonight's camp.'"
4.4: Joshua summoned the twelve men he had set aside — one from each tribe of Israel —
4.5: and said: 'Go back across ahead of the ark of the LORD your God into the middle of the Jordan. Each man, lift a stone onto your shoulder — one for each of the twelve tribes of Israel.'
4.6: 'These stones will be a sign among you. In days to come, when your children ask: "What are these stones for?"'
4.7: 'answer them: the Jordan's water was cut off before the ark of the covenant of the LORD — when the ark passed over, the water stopped. These stones will be a lasting memorial for Israel in every generation.'
4.8: The Israelites did exactly as Joshua commanded. They lifted twelve stones from the middle of the Jordan — one for each tribe, as the LORD had directed Joshua — carried them to the camp, and laid them down there.
4.9: Joshua also set up a second set of twelve stones in the middle of the Jordan itself — at the precise place where the feet of the priests who bore the ark had stood. They remain there to this day.
4.10: The priests stood in the middle of the Jordan until every command the LORD had given Joshua was fulfilled — everything Moses had charged Joshua to do. The people crossed quickly.
4.11: Once all the people had crossed, the ark of the LORD and the priests crossed over before the people to the other side.
4.12: The Reubenites, Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh crossed over in full battle formation ahead of the Israelites, just as Moses had directed them.
4.13: About forty thousand warriors, armed for war, marched before the LORD onto the plains of Jericho.
4.14: That day the LORD made Joshua great in the eyes of all Israel, and they stood in awe of him as they had stood in awe of Moses — all his life long.
4.16: "Tell the priests carrying the ark of testimony to come up out of the Jordan."
4.17: Joshua gave the command: "Come up out of the Jordan!"
4.18: The moment the feet of the priests bearing the ark of the covenant came up out of the Jordan and touched dry ground, the Jordan's waters poured back in, overflowing all its banks just as they had before.
4.19: The people came up from the Jordan on the tenth day of the first month and made camp at Gilgal, on the eastern edge of Jericho's plain.
4.20: There at Gilgal, Joshua erected the twelve stones they had brought up from the Jordan.
4.21: He said to the Israelites: 'In the days to come, when your children ask their fathers, "What are these stones for?"'
4.22: 'tell them: "Here Israel crossed the Jordan on dry ground."'
4.23: 'For the LORD your God dried up the Jordan ahead of you until you had crossed — just as he dried up the Red Sea for us until we crossed over.'
4.24: 'so that every people on earth would know that the LORD's hand is mighty — and so that you would fear the LORD your God for all time.'

5.1: When all the Amorite kings west of the Jordan and all the Canaanite kings along the coast heard how the LORD had dried up the Jordan before the Israelites until they crossed, every last ounce of resolve drained out of them. No one had the will to resist Israel.
5.2: At that moment the LORD said to Joshua: 'Make flint knives and circumcise the Israelites — restore the covenant sign.'
5.3: Joshua made flint knives and circumcised all the Israelite men at Gibeath-haaraloth — the Hill of the Foreskins.
5.4: Here is why Joshua had to circumcise them: every man who had come out of Egypt — every fighting man of that generation — had died in the wilderness on the journey from Egypt.
5.5: Everyone who left Egypt had been circumcised, but the children born during the forty years in the wilderness had not received the covenant sign.
5.6: Israel had wandered in the wilderness forty years until the entire generation of soldiers who left Egypt was gone — they had not obeyed the LORD's voice. So the LORD had sworn they would never see the land he had promised to give their ancestors: a land flowing with milk and honey.
5.7: Their sons had taken their place, and it was these Joshua now circumcised — they had gone without the covenant sign all through the journey.
5.8: After every man had been circumcised, they stayed in the camp and recovered until they were whole.
5.9: The LORD said to Joshua: 'Today I have rolled away the shame of Egypt from you.' And so that place has been called Gilgal — meaning 'rolled' — to this day.
5.10: Israel camped at Gilgal and kept the Passover — at evening on the fourteenth day of the month — out on the plains of Jericho.
5.11: The morning after the Passover — that same day — they ate from the land's own produce: flat unleavened bread and roasted grain.
5.12: The manna stopped the morning after they ate from the land. There was no more manna for Israel from that day forward. That year they ate what the land of Canaan itself produced.
5.13: While Joshua was near Jericho, he looked up and saw a man standing right before him with his sword drawn. Joshua walked toward him and said: 'Whose side are you on — ours or our enemies'?'
5.14: 'Neither,' the man replied. 'I come as Commander of the LORD's army.' Joshua fell face to the ground and bowed in worship, then said: 'What command does my lord have for his servant?'
5.15: The Commander of the LORD's army said: 'Remove your sandal from your foot — the ground where you stand is holy ground.' Joshua obeyed.

6.1: Jericho was sealed tight on account of Israel — the gates were barred, no one going in, no one coming out.
6.2: Then the LORD said to Joshua: 'Look — I have handed Jericho over to you, its king, and all its warriors.'
6.3: 'Have all the soldiers march around the city — once around, each day. Do this for six days.'
6.4: 'Seven priests are to carry seven rams'-horn trumpets ahead of the ark. On the seventh day march around the city seven times, with the priests sounding the trumpets throughout.'
6.5: 'When the ram's horn gives a long final blast and you hear it, let the whole army shout — a full battle cry. The wall of the city will crumble, and every man will charge straight in.'
6.6: Joshua son of Nun called for the priests and gave them their orders: 'Lift the ark of the covenant. Let seven priests carry seven rams'-horn trumpets ahead of the LORD's ark.'
6.7: Then he told the troops: 'Forward! March around the city. The armed vanguard goes ahead of the LORD's ark.'
6.8: As Joshua gave the word, the seven priests moved forward before the LORD, blowing their seven rams'-horn trumpets, and the ark of the LORD's covenant followed after them.
6.9: The armed vanguard led the way before the priests blowing the trumpets; the rear guard came after the ark — and the trumpets sounded without stopping.
6.10: Joshua had given this command to the troops: 'No shouting, no noise, not a single word — until I tell you to shout. When I give the command, then shout.'
6.11: So the ark of the LORD was carried around the city once. Then they returned to camp and spent the night.
6.12: Early the next morning Joshua was up, and the priests lifted the ark of the LORD.
6.13: The seven priests with their seven rams'-horn trumpets marched before the LORD's ark, blowing steadily all the way. The armed troops went ahead; the rear guard followed the ark — and the trumpets never stopped.
6.14: On the second day they circled the city once and returned to camp. This was the pattern for six days.
6.15: On the seventh day they rose at first light and circled the city seven times in the same way. Only on this day did they circle it seven times.
6.16: At the seventh circuit, as the priests gave the final trumpet blast, Joshua cried out to the army: 'Shout! The LORD has given you this city!'
6.17: 'The city and all it contains are given over to the LORD — placed under the sacred ban of destruction. Only Rahab the prostitute is to live, and everyone in her house with her, because she hid the men we sent.'
6.18: 'Stay away from everything under the ban — not a single item. If you take anything devoted to destruction, you will bring that same destruction down on the camp of Israel and plunge it into disaster.'
6.19: 'All the silver, gold, bronze, and iron belong to the LORD — they are holy to him and must go into his treasury.'
6.20: The priests sounded the trumpets, the people gave a great battle cry — and the wall crumbled to the ground. Every soldier charged straight in and they seized the city.
6.21: They carried out the sacred ban against everything in the city — men and women, young and old, cattle, sheep, and donkeys — all put to the sword.
6.22: Joshua told the two men who had scouted the land: 'Go to the house of the prostitute and bring out the woman and everyone with her, just as you swore to her.'
6.23: The young men who had served as spies went in and brought out Rahab, her father, her mother, her brothers, and all who were hers — her entire household. They placed them safely outside the camp of Israel.
6.24: They burned the city to the ground with everything in it. The silver, gold, bronze, and iron they brought to the treasury of the LORD's house.
6.25: Rahab the prostitute, her father's household, and all who belonged to her — Joshua spared them all. She lived among the Israelites from that day forward, because she had hidden the messengers Joshua sent to spy out Jericho.
6.26: Joshua then placed this solemn curse on the city: 'Let the man who rebuilds this city Jericho be cursed before the LORD. He will lay its first stone at the cost of his firstborn son, and set up its gates at the cost of his youngest.'
6.27: The LORD was with Joshua, and his name became known across the whole land.

7.1: But Israel broke faith over the sacred ban: Achan son of Carmi, son of Zabdi, son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, had taken items devoted to destruction. The LORD's anger blazed against Israel.
7.2: Joshua sent scouts from Jericho to Ai — the town near Beth-aven, east of Bethel — with orders: 'Go, scout the territory.' The men went and reconnoitered Ai.
7.3: 'Not everyone needs to march,' they reported back to Joshua. 'Send two or three thousand — Ai is small. There's no need to exhaust the whole army.'
7.4: About three thousand soldiers went up — and they broke and ran before Ai's men.
7.5: Ai's defenders cut down about thirty-six Israelites, chasing them from the gate all the way to Shebarim, cutting them down on the descent. The hearts of the people dissolved like water.
7.6: Joshua tore his garments and fell face to the ground before the ark of the LORD and lay there until evening — he and the elders of Israel, with dust heaped on their heads.
7.7: 'O Lord GOD — why did you ever lead this people across the Jordan? To hand us over to the Amorites and wipe us out? We should have settled on the other side and never crossed!'
7.8: 'What can I say, Lord, when Israel turns its back and runs from its enemies?'
7.9: 'The Canaanites and every people in the land will hear of this and close in around us, wiping our name from the earth. And then what becomes of your great name?'
7.10: The LORD said to Joshua: 'Get up! Why are you lying face-down like this?'
7.11: 'Israel has sinned. They have broken the covenant I commanded — they took from the sacred ban, they stole, they lied, and they buried the plunder among their own things.'
7.12: 'That is why Israel cannot face its enemies — it turns and flees, because the camp itself has fallen under the curse of the sacred ban. I will not be with you anymore until you remove that banned thing from your midst.'
7.13: 'Rise up. Consecrate the people. Tell them: Prepare yourselves for tomorrow — for the LORD God of Israel says: There is a banned thing in your midst, Israel. You cannot stand before your enemies until you rid yourselves of it.'
7.14: In the morning you will present yourselves tribe by tribe. The tribe the LORD selects comes forward clan by clan; the clan selected comes forward household by household; the household selected comes forward man by man.
7.15: 'Whoever is found holding the banned goods will be burned — he and all he owns — because he violated the LORD's covenant and committed an outrage against Israel.'
7.16: Early the next morning Joshua brought all Israel forward tribe by tribe, and the tribe of Judah was singled out.
7.17: The clans of Judah came forward, and the Zerahites were singled out. The Zerahites came forward man by man, and Zabdi was singled out.
7.18: Zabdi's household came forward man by man — and Achan son of Carmi, son of Zabdi, son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, was singled out.
7.19: Joshua said to Achan: 'My son, give glory and honor to the LORD, the God of Israel. Tell me truthfully what you have done — hide nothing from me.'
7.20: Achan answered Joshua: 'Yes, it is true. I sinned against the LORD God of Israel. And this is what I did.'
7.21: 'I spotted a fine cloak from Babylon among the plunder — and two hundred shekels of silver and a fifty-shekel gold bar. I wanted them. I took them. They are buried in the ground inside my tent, with the silver at the bottom.'
7.22: Joshua sent men running to the tent. There it was — buried in Achan's tent, with the silver underneath, exactly as he had said.
7.23: They took the stolen goods from inside the tent and brought them to Joshua and all Israel and laid them out before the LORD.
7.24: Joshua and all Israel seized Achan son of Zerah — along with the silver, the cloak, the gold bar, his sons and daughters, his cattle, donkeys, and sheep, his tent and every possession — and brought them all to the Valley of Achor.
7.25: Joshua said: 'Why have you brought disaster on us? The LORD will bring disaster on you this very day.' All Israel stoned Achan to death, and afterward they were burned with fire.
7.26: They heaped a great pile of stones over him, and it stands to this day. Then the LORD's blazing anger subsided. And so that place was named the Valley of Achor — 'Trouble' — a name it carries to this day.

8.1: The LORD said to Joshua: 'Do not be afraid; do not be disheartened. Take the whole fighting force and go up to Ai. I am handing over to you its king, its people, its city, and its land.'
8.2: 'Deal with Ai and its king the same way you dealt with Jericho and its king — but this time, take the livestock and goods for yourselves. Set an ambush behind the city.'
8.3: Joshua and the whole army prepared to advance against Ai. Joshua selected thirty thousand of his best warriors and dispatched them under cover of darkness.
8.4: 'Here are your orders,' he said. 'You will take position in ambush behind the city. Stay close — do not go far — and keep every man ready.'
8.5: 'I will approach the city with all my troops. When they come out to meet us the same as last time, we will turn and run.'
8.6: 'They will chase us until we have pulled them clear of the city — they will be thinking, "Israel is running again, just like last time." And we will run.'
8.7: 'Then you spring from ambush and take the city — the LORD your God is handing it to you.'
8.8: 'Once it falls, burn it. This is the LORD's command — and I am passing it on to you as your orders.'
8.9: Joshua sent the ambush force ahead, and they took position between Bethel and Ai, on the west side of Ai, and waited. Joshua himself spent that night with the main camp.
8.10: At first light Joshua mustered his forces. He and the elders of Israel led the army up toward Ai.
8.11: The entire fighting force advanced and took up position on the north side of Ai — a valley lay between them and the city.
8.12: He also placed about five thousand men in ambush between Bethel and Ai on the city's west side.
8.13: With the main force to the north and the ambush force to the west, Joshua moved into the valley that night.
8.14: The king of Ai saw them. He and all his people rushed out at daybreak to engage Israel on the plain at the regular battle site — not knowing that a force lay hidden behind the city.
8.15: Joshua and all Israel feigned defeat and fled toward the desert.
8.16: Every man in Ai was rallied to give chase. They poured out after Joshua, drawn away from the city.
8.17: Not one man remained in Ai or Bethel — they all poured out in pursuit of Israel, leaving the city gates wide open and unguarded.
8.18: The LORD said to Joshua: 'Aim the spear in your hand at Ai — I am giving it to you.' Joshua raised his spear toward the city.
8.19: The moment Joshua's hand shot forward, the ambush force sprang from their position and rushed into the city. They seized Ai and immediately put it to the torch.
8.20: Ai's soldiers turned and looked — smoke from the city was billowing up to heaven. They had nowhere to run. The Israelites who had fled into the desert wheeled around to face them.
8.21: When Joshua and all Israel saw the smoke rising — proof the ambush had taken the city — they turned and cut down Ai's men.
8.22: The men from the city came out to meet them — and now Ai's army was caught in the middle, Israelites on both sides. They were cut down until not a single man survived.
8.23: The king of Ai they captured alive and brought to Joshua.
8.24: Once Israel had finished killing all of Ai's men — cut down in the open fields and in the desert where they had fled, every one falling by the sword — all Israel came back to Ai and put it to the sword.
8.25: The dead that day — men and women together — numbered twelve thousand. The entire population of Ai was gone.
8.26: Joshua held his spear outstretched toward the city — as Moses had held his staff at Rephidim — until every man and woman of Ai had been devoted to destruction.
8.27: Israel took only the livestock and the spoil of the city — their lawful share, as the LORD had directed Joshua.
8.28: Joshua burned Ai and left it a permanent rubble-heap — a desolation that stands to this day.
8.29: The king of Ai was hanged on a tree until sunset. At sundown Joshua ordered the body taken down and thrown at the city gate, and a great heap of stones was piled over it — it remains there to this day.
8.30: Then Joshua built an altar to the LORD God of Israel on Mount Ebal,
8.31: exactly as Moses the LORD's servant had commanded Israel, as prescribed in the Book of the Torah of Moses: an altar of unworked stone, no iron tool touching it. They offered burnt offerings to the LORD and sacrificed peace offerings — fulfilling Deuteronomy 27 at the moment of first settlement.
8.32: There Joshua inscribed on the stones a full copy of the Torah of Moses — written out in the sight of all Israel.
8.33: All Israel — native-born and foreigner alike — stood in two groups with their elders, officers, and judges, facing the Levitical priests who carried the LORD's ark. Half stood before Mount Gerizim, half before Mount Ebal, exactly as Moses the LORD's servant had prescribed for pronouncing blessing over the people.
8.34: Afterward Joshua read aloud every word of the Torah — the blessings and the curses — everything written in the Book of the Law.
8.35: Not a single word of all that Moses had commanded was left out — Joshua read it all before the whole assembly of Israel: men, women, children, and the foreigners dwelling in their midst.

9.1: When the news spread to all the kings west of the Jordan — in the highlands, the foothills, and along the entire Mediterranean coast up to Lebanon — all the Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites
9.2: joined forces with one purpose: to make war on Joshua and Israel.
9.3: The people of Gibeon took a different course. They heard what Joshua had done to Jericho and Ai,
9.4: and came up with a scheme. They disguised themselves as distant ambassadors, loading worn-out sacks on their donkeys and taking cracked, patched wineskins,
9.5: wearing patched, worn-out sandals and threadbare clothing, with bread that was dry and moldy.
9.6: They came to Joshua at the camp at Gilgal and said to him and to the men of Israel: 'We have come from a very distant land. Make a treaty with us.'
9.7: The Israelites said to the Hivites: 'What if you actually live right among us? Then we could make no treaty with you.'
9.8: 'We are your servants,' they said. Joshua asked them: 'Who are you? Where do you come from?'
9.9: 'Your servants have come from a very distant land, drawn by the fame of the LORD your God. We heard the report of his name and everything he did in Egypt,'
9.10: 'and what he did to the two Amorite kings beyond the Jordan — Sihon of Heshbon and Og of Bashan at Ashtaroth.'
9.11: 'So our elders and all our people sent us with this charge: "Take provisions for the journey, go meet them, and say: We are your servants — make a treaty with us."'
9.12: 'Look at this bread — it was still warm from the oven when we left home to come to you. Look at it now: dry and crumbled.'
9.13: 'These wineskins were new when we filled them — look at them now, cracked and burst. Our clothing and sandals are worn through from the sheer length of the journey.'
9.14: The men of Israel tasted their provisions — and did not seek the LORD's word.
9.15: Joshua made peace with them and concluded a treaty — he would let them live — and the leaders of the assembly swore an oath to them. The oath was binding.
9.16: Three days after the treaty was sealed, the Israelites learned that these men were their neighbors — they lived right in the same region.
9.17: The Israelites marched and reached these cities on the third day: Gibeon, Chephirah, Beeroth, and Kiriath-jearim.
9.18: But Israel held back its sword, because the assembly's leaders had sworn an oath by the LORD God of Israel — the oath was binding even though obtained by deception. The whole community erupted in complaint against the leaders.
9.19: The leaders answered the whole assembly: 'We gave them a sworn oath in the name of the LORD God of Israel — we cannot lay a hand on them.'
9.20: 'We will let them live. If we break the sworn oath, God's wrath will fall on us — and we cannot have that.'
9.21: 'Let them live,' the leaders ruled. So the Gibeonites became hewers of wood and drawers of water for the whole community — the role the leaders had assigned them.
9.22: Joshua summoned the Gibeonites and confronted them: 'Why did you deceive us — claiming to come from far away when you live right here among us?'
9.23: 'You are therefore cursed — none of your people will ever be free from serving as woodcutters and water-carriers for the house of my God.'
9.24: They answered Joshua: 'We were told plainly how the LORD your God commanded his servant Moses to give you this whole land and to destroy all its inhabitants. We feared for our very lives — that is why we did what we did.'
9.25: 'We are in your hands. Do with us whatever seems right and good to you.'
9.26: That is what Joshua did — he rescued them from the Israelites' sword.
9.27: Joshua assigned them that day as woodcutters and water-carriers for the community and for the LORD's altar — serving wherever the LORD would choose. And so it has remained to this day.

10.1: Adonizedek king of Jerusalem heard that Joshua had captured Ai and devoted it to destruction — treating Ai and its king the same way he had treated Jericho — and that the Gibeonites had made peace with Israel and were living among them.
10.2: This alarmed him greatly. Gibeon was a major city — one of the royal cities — larger than Ai, with an army of skilled warriors.
10.3: Adonizedek king of Jerusalem sent to Hoham king of Hebron, Piram king of Jarmuth, Japhia king of Lachish, and Debir king of Eglon with this message:
10.4: 'Come up and help me strike Gibeon — they have made peace with Joshua and Israel.'
10.5: The five Amorite kings — of Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, and Eglon — united their armies, marched out together, besieged Gibeon, and attacked.
10.6: The men of Gibeon sent an urgent message to Joshua at Gilgal: 'Do not abandon your servants! Come quickly — rescue us! All the Amorite kings from the highlands have united against us!'
10.7: Joshua marched up from Gilgal — the whole fighting force with him, every seasoned warrior.
10.8: The LORD said to Joshua: 'Do not be afraid of them — I have handed them to you. Not one man will be able to stand before you.'
10.9: Joshua marched all night from Gilgal and fell on them without warning.
10.10: The LORD threw them into confusion before Israel. Israel struck them a massive blow at Gibeon and chased them up the pass of Beth-horon, cutting them down all the way to Azekah and Makkedah.
10.11: As the enemy fled down the Beth-horon pass, the LORD hurled massive hailstones on them from the sky — all the way to Azekah. More died from the hail than were killed by Israelite swords.
10.12: That day — the day the LORD handed the Amorites to Israel — Joshua spoke to the LORD in the hearing of all Israel and said: 'Sun, be still over Gibeon! Moon, hold your place in the Valley of Aijalon!'
10.13: The sun was still and the moon held its place until the nation had taken full vengeance on its enemies. This is recorded in the Book of Jashar. The sun stood fixed in the middle of the sky and did not hasten to go down for nearly a full day.
10.14: There has never been a day like it — before or since — when the LORD responded to a human voice like that. For the LORD himself was fighting for Israel.
10.15: Joshua and all Israel returned to the camp at Gilgal.
10.16: The five kings had fled and hidden themselves in a cave at Makkedah.
10.17: Word came to Joshua: 'The five kings have been found — hiding in the cave at Makkedah.'
10.18: 'Roll large stones over the cave mouth,' Joshua ordered, 'and set guards there.'
10.19: 'But the rest of you — keep pursuing! Cut off the retreat. Don't let them get back to their cities. The LORD your God has handed them to you.'
10.20: When Joshua and the Israelites had finished the slaughter — a crushing blow — and whatever survivors remained had scrambled into their fortified cities,
10.21: the whole army returned in peace to Joshua at Makkedah — not a man dared breathe a threat against Israel.
10.22: 'Open the cave and bring the five kings out to me,' Joshua ordered.
10.23: They did so, and the five kings were brought out from the cave: the king of Jerusalem, the king of Hebron, the king of Jarmuth, the king of Lachish, and the king of Eglon.
10.24: When the kings were brought out before him, Joshua summoned all Israel and said to his commanding officers: 'Come and put your feet on the necks of these kings.' They came forward and placed their feet on the kings' necks.
10.25: 'Do not be afraid; do not lose heart,' Joshua said to them. 'Be strong and full of courage — this is what the LORD will do to every enemy you face.'
10.26: After that Joshua had them killed and their bodies hung on five trees, where they hung until sunset.
10.27: At sunset Joshua gave the order: the bodies were taken down from the trees and thrown into the cave where the kings had hidden. Large stones were placed over the entrance — and they remain there to this very day.
10.28: That day Joshua took Makkedah — put it to the sword, and devoted its king and every soul in it to destruction. Not one was left. He dealt with Makkedah's king as he had dealt with Jericho's.
10.29: Joshua and all Israel moved from Makkedah to Libnah and attacked it.
10.30: The LORD gave Libnah and its king into Israel's hand. Joshua put it and every person in it to the sword — not one survivor. He dealt with its king as he had dealt with Jericho's.
10.31: From Libnah, Joshua and all Israel moved to Lachish, laid siege to it, and attacked.
10.32: The LORD gave Lachish to Israel. Joshua took it on the second day and put every person to the sword, just as he had done to Libnah.
10.33: King Horam of Gezer came up to relieve Lachish, but Joshua struck him down along with his whole army — not one survivor.
10.34: From Lachish, Joshua and all Israel moved on to Eglon, surrounded it, and attacked.
10.35: They captured Eglon that day, put it to the sword, and devoted every soul in it to destruction — just as they had done to Lachish.
10.36: From Eglon, Joshua and all Israel went up to Hebron and attacked it.
10.37: They took Hebron — put the city, its king, all its surrounding towns, and every person to the sword. Not one survivor remained, just as at Eglon. Every soul was devoted to destruction.
10.38: Joshua and all Israel wheeled back to Debir and attacked it.
10.39: He took Debir and its king and surrounding towns, put everyone to the sword, and devoted all to destruction — not one survivor. He treated Debir and its king just as he had treated Hebron, Libnah, and their kings.
10.40: Joshua thus conquered the entire region — the highlands, the Negev, the western foothills, and the mountain slopes — and all their kings. He left not one alive; everything that breathed was devoted to destruction, as the LORD God of Israel had commanded.
10.41: Joshua's campaign extended from Kadesh-barnea to Gaza, across all the region of Goshen, up to Gibeon.
10.42: All these kings and their lands Joshua took in a single campaign, for the LORD God of Israel was fighting for Israel.
10.43: Joshua and all Israel then returned to the camp at Gilgal.

11.1: When Jabin king of Hazor heard the news, he sent word to Jobab king of Madon, and to the kings of Shimron and Achshaph,
11.2: and to the kings of the northern highlands, the Arabah south of Kinnereth, the western lowlands, and the heights of Dor on the coast,
11.3: and to the Canaanites east and west, the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, and Jebusites in the highlands, and the Hivites below Mount Hermon in the land of Mizpah.
11.4: They mustered their forces — a vast army, as countless as the sand on the seashore — with horses and chariots in great number.
11.5: All these kings assembled and marched out together to the Waters of Merom to make war on Israel.
11.6: The LORD said to Joshua: 'Do not be afraid of them. By this time tomorrow I will have delivered all of them, slain, to Israel. Hamstring their horses and burn their chariots.'
11.7: Joshua and all his fighting men fell on them suddenly at the Waters of Merom.
11.8: The LORD gave them into Israel's hand. Israel cut them down and chased the survivors as far as Greater Sidon and Misrephoth-maim, and east to the Valley of Mizpah — striking them down until not one remained.
11.9: Joshua did exactly as the LORD directed: he hamstrung the horses and burned the chariots.
11.10: Joshua then turned back and captured Hazor, putting its king to the sword. Hazor had been the dominant power over all those kingdoms.
11.11: They put every soul in it to the sword and devoted them to destruction — nothing that breathed was left alive. Then Joshua burned Hazor to the ground.
11.12: Joshua captured all the cities of those kings and all their kings, putting them to the sword and devoting them to destruction, exactly as Moses the LORD's servant had commanded.
11.13: Israel did not burn the cities still standing on their ancient mounds — only Hazor, which Joshua burned.
11.14: Israel took all the spoil and livestock from these cities as their own. But every human being was put to the sword until every last one was destroyed — nothing that breathed was left.
11.15: The LORD commanded Moses; Moses commanded Joshua; Joshua obeyed. He left nothing undone of all that the LORD had commanded Moses.
11.16: Joshua took the whole land: the highlands, all the Negev, all the land of Goshen, the western lowlands, the Arabah, and the highlands and foothills of Israel —
11.17: from Mount Halak rising toward Seir all the way north to Baal-gad in the Lebanon Valley below Mount Hermon. Every king he captured, he struck down and executed.
11.18: The war against all these kings was long.
11.19: Not a single city made peace with Israel — except the Hivites of Gibeon. Every other city was taken by force.
11.20: For it was the LORD's design that their hearts be hardened to make war on Israel — so they would be devoted to destruction with no mercy shown, exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses. The outcome was never in doubt.
11.21: At that time Joshua destroyed the Anakites from the highlands — from Hebron, Debir, Anab, and all the highlands of Judah and Israel. Joshua devoted them and their cities to destruction.
11.22: Not one Anakim remained in Israelite territory. Some survived in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod — and from there their line would eventually produce Goliath and the Philistine giants.
11.23: Joshua took the whole land — exactly as the LORD had spoken to Moses — and gave it to Israel as their inheritance according to their tribal divisions. And the land rested from war.

12.1: Here are the kings of the land whom Israel struck down and whose territory they possessed east of the Jordan — from the Arnon River to Mount Hermon, along with all the eastern Arabah:
12.2: Sihon, Amorite king at Heshbon, whose domain ran from Aroer on the Arnon's bank — and the river's midchannel — across half of Gilead to the Jabbok, the Ammonite border;
12.3: and the eastern Arabah from the Sea of Kinnereth down to the Dead Sea, along the road to Beth-jeshimoth and south along the slopes of Pisgah;
12.4: and the territory of Og king of Bashan — one of the last survivors of the Rephaim — who ruled from Ashtaroth and Edrei:
12.5: He ruled over Mount Hermon, Salecah, all of Bashan to the borders of Geshur and Maacah, and half of Gilead to Sihon's border.
12.6: Moses the LORD's servant and the Israelites defeated them. Moses the LORD's servant gave their land to the Reubenites, Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh as their inheritance.
12.7: And these are the kings west of the Jordan whom Joshua and Israel defeated — from Baal-gad in the Lebanon Valley south to Mount Halak rising toward Seir. Joshua gave their land to the tribes of Israel as their inheritance by tribal divisions:
12.8: the highlands, the foothills, the Arabah, the slopes, the desert, and the Negev — formerly the territory of the Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites:
12.9: the king of Jericho, one; the king of Ai beside Bethel, one;
12.10: the king of Jerusalem, one; the king of Hebron, one;
12.11: the king of Jarmuth, one; the king of Lachish, one;
12.12: the king of Eglon, one; the king of Gezer, one;
12.13: the king of Debir, one; the king of Geder, one;
12.14: the king of Hormah, one; the king of Arad, one;
12.15: the king of Libnah, one; the king of Adullam, one;
12.16: the king of Makkedah, one; the king of Bethel, one;
12.17: the king of Tappuah, one; the king of Hepher, one;
12.18: the king of Aphek, one; the king of Lasharon, one;
12.19: the king of Madon, one; the king of Hazor, one;
12.20: the king of Shimron-meron, one; the king of Achshaph, one;
12.21: the king of Taanach, one; the king of Megiddo, one;
12.22: the king of Kedesh, one; the king of Jokneam in Carmel, one;
12.23: the king of Dor in the heights of Dor, one; the king of Goiim at Gilgal, one;
12.24: the king of Tirzah, one. Thirty-one kings in all.

13.1: By the time Joshua was old and far along in years, the LORD said to him: 'You are old, and much of your life is behind you — yet a great deal of land remains to be possessed.'
13.2: This is what has not yet been taken: the entire Philistine country and all the territory of the Geshurites,
13.3: from the Shihor River east of Egypt all the way north to the border of Ekron — territory reckoned as Canaanite — covering the five Philistine city-lords ruling Gaza, Ashdod, Ashkelon, Gath, and Ekron; and the Avvim
13.4: to the south — all the Canaanite territory, including the cave-country of Mearah belonging to the Sidonians, extending to Aphek at the Amorite frontier,
13.5: the Gebalite territories and all of Lebanon stretching east — from Baalgad at the foot of Mount Hermon all the way to Lebo-hamath in the north,
13.6: every person settled in the highlands from Lebanon to Misrephoth-maim — all the Sidonians. I myself will drive them out from before Israel. For now, your task is simply to allot the land to Israel as I commanded you.
13.7: So divide this land now and grant it as a lasting inheritance to the nine tribes and the half-tribe of Manasseh.
13.8: The Reubenites, Gadites, and the other half-tribe of Manasseh had already received their portion — Moses had assigned their inheritance east of the Jordan, exactly as the LORD's servant Moses had granted it:
13.9: starting from Aroer on the rim of the Arnon Gorge — including the town in the gorge itself — along the entire plateau of Medeba as far as Dibon,
13.10: and all the cities that had been Sihon's — the Amorite king who once ruled from Heshbon — extending to the Ammonite border,
13.11: and Gilead, the Geshurite and Maacathite territories, all of Mount Hermon, and all of Bashan as far north as Salecah —
13.12: the whole kingdom of Og in Bashan, who had ruled from Ashtaroth and Edrei — one of the last survivors of the Rephaim. Moses struck them down and drove them out.
13.13: However, Israel never expelled the Geshurites or the Maacathites — they have lived among Israel's people from that day to this.
13.14: The tribe of Levi received no territorial inheritance. Their inheritance was the LORD's own offerings made by fire — the LORD God of Israel himself had declared this would be their portion.
13.15: Moses assigned the tribal inheritance of Reuben to their various clans.
13.16: Their territory stretched from Aroer on the rim of the Arnon Gorge — including the town in the gorge's heart — across the entire plateau near Medeba,
13.17: Heshbon with its outlying towns on the plateau — Dibon, Bamoth-baal, Beth-baal-meon,
13.19: Kiriathaim, Sibmah, Zereth-shahar on the valley ridge,
13.20: Beth-peor, the Pisgah slopes, and Beth-jeshimoth —
13.21: all the plateau towns, the whole domain of Sihon the Amorite king of Heshbon — Moses struck him down along with the Midianite chieftains Evi, Rekem, Zur, Hur, and Reba, nobles who had lived in that land under Sihon.
13.22: Israel also killed Balaam son of Beor — the diviner — by the sword, among all the others who fell.
13.23: The Jordan River and its bank formed Reuben's western border. Such was the inheritance of Reuben by clan — their towns and villages.
13.24: Moses assigned the inheritance of Gad to their clans.
13.25: Gad's territory took in Jazer and all the towns of Gilead, the half of Ammonite land up to Aroer east of Rabbah,
13.26: stretching from Heshbon to Ramath-mizpah and Betonim, from Mahanaim to the Debir boundary,
13.27: and in the rift valley: Beth-haram, Beth-nimrah, Succoth, and Zaphon — the remaining portions of Sihon's kingdom — bounded by the Jordan to the southern tip of the Sea of Chinnereth on the east side of the river.
13.28: Such was Gad's inheritance by clan — their towns and villages.
13.29: Moses assigned the inheritance of the half-tribe of Manasseh to their clans.
13.30: Their domain began at Mahanaim and extended through all of Bashan — Og's entire former kingdom — including all sixty towns of Jair in Bashan.
13.31: Half of Gilead, plus Ashtaroth and Edrei — Og's royal cities in Bashan — went to the descendants of Machir son of Manasseh, to half their number, by their clans.
13.32: These were the allotments Moses made in the plains of Moab — east of the Jordan, across from Jericho.
13.33: But Moses gave the tribe of Levi no land at all. The LORD God of Israel himself is their inheritance — just as he promised them. No borders, no cities, no territory: only God.

14.1: These are the portions the Israelites inherited in Canaan — distributed by Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the heads of the ancestral families of Israel's tribes.
14.2: The lots fell as the LORD had commanded through Moses — distributing the land among the nine and a half tribes.
14.3: Moses had already settled the two and a half tribes east of the Jordan, and he had given the Levites no territorial inheritance at all.
14.4: Joseph's line had split into two tribes — Manasseh and Ephraim — which kept the tribal count at twelve even without Levi. The Levites received no territory, only towns to live in with pasturelands for their livestock.
14.5: Israel carried out what the LORD had commanded Moses, and the land was divided.
14.6: Then the tribe of Judah came to Joshua at Gilgal. Caleb son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite stepped forward and said: 'You know the word the LORD spoke to Moses the man of God concerning you and me at Kadesh-barnea.'
14.7: I was forty years old when Moses the LORD's servant sent me from Kadesh-barnea to scout out the land. I came back and gave him my honest report — I told him exactly what I believed.
14.8: My fellow spies drained the courage out of the whole people — but I followed the LORD my God with my whole heart.
14.9: That very day Moses swore an oath: 'The land your feet have walked will belong to you and your descendants as a permanent inheritance — because you followed the LORD my God with your whole heart.'
14.10: And look — the LORD has kept me alive exactly as he promised. Forty-five years have passed since the LORD spoke that word to Moses, all through Israel's wilderness years. Today I am eighty-five years old!
14.11: My strength is the same today as it was when Moses sent me. I can still march, still fight, still lead — just as I could at forty.
14.12: So now — give me this mountain! The LORD spoke about it on that day, and you yourself heard how the Anakim were there with their great walled cities. If the LORD is with me — as I know he will be — I will drive them out, just as he promised.
14.13: Joshua blessed him and gave Hebron to Caleb son of Jephunneh as his inheritance.
14.14: Hebron has been Caleb son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite's inheritance from that day to this — because he followed the LORD God of Israel with his whole heart.
14.15: Hebron had once been called Kiriath-arba — 'City of Arba' — after Arba, the greatest warrior among the Anakim. And the land had rest from war.

15.1: This was the allotment for the tribe of Judah by their clans: along the Edomite border in the south, with the Wilderness of Zin marking the southernmost edge.
15.2: The southern boundary began at the southern bay of the Dead Sea.
15.3: running south of the Ascent of Scorpions, crossing through Zin, passing south of Kadesh-barnea, continuing along to Hezron, up to Addar, curving around to Karka,
15.4: on to Azmon, then out along the Wadi of Egypt and ending at the sea. That was their southern line.
15.5: The eastern boundary ran along the Dead Sea to the mouth of the Jordan. The northern boundary started from the sea's bay at the Jordan's outlet.
15.6: The border ran up to Beth-hoglah, passed north of Beth-arabah, and climbed to the Stone of Bohan son of Reuben.
15.7: From the Valley of Achor the line climbed toward Debir, then turned northward toward Gilgal — opposite the Ascent of Adummim on the valley's south slope — passing the springs of En-shemesh and ending at En-rogel.
15.8: From there the border climbed through the Valley of Ben-hinnom to the south slope of Jebusite Jerusalem, then up to the hilltop west of the Hinnom Valley — at the northern end of the Valley of Rephaim.
15.9: From the hilltop the line ran to the spring at Nephtoah, to the towns of Mount Ephron, and on to Baalah — the same place as Kiriath-jearim.
15.10: From Baalah the line turned west to Mount Seir, ran along the north flank of Mount Jearim — that is Chesalon — descended to Beth-shemesh, and continued on to Timnah.
15.11: The border continued to the north side of Ekron, swung around to Shikkeron, along Mount Baalah to Jabneel — and the sea marked the end.
15.12: The western border was simply the Mediterranean Sea. These were Judah's boundaries on every side, by their clans.
15.13: Following the LORD's command to Joshua, Caleb son of Jephunneh received his portion within Judah — Kiriath-arba, city of Arba the ancestor of Anak, which is Hebron.
15.14: Caleb drove out the three sons of Anak from Hebron — Sheshai, Ahiman, and Talmai.
15.15: From Hebron he marched against Debir — once called Kiriath-sepher, the City of the Scroll.
15.16: Caleb announced: 'Whoever strikes Kiriath-sepher and takes it, I will give him my daughter Achsah as his wife.'
15.17: Othniel son of Kenaz — Caleb's own brother — took the city. Caleb gave him Achsah as promised.
15.18: When Achsah arrived, she persuaded Othniel to ask her father for farmland. She slipped off her donkey, and Caleb asked her: 'What do you need?'
15.19: 'Give me a blessing,' she said. 'You've given me the Negev — arid land. Give me the springs of water too.' So he gave her both the upper springs and the lower springs.
15.20: Such was the inheritance of Judah by their clans.
15.21: Judah's southernmost towns, near the Edomite border, included: Kabzeel, Eder, Jagur,
15.32: Lebaoth, Shilhim, Ain, and Rimmon. Twenty-nine towns in all, with their villages.
15.33: In the foothills of the Shephelah: Eshtaol, Zorah, Ashnah,
15.36: Shaaraim, Adithaim, Gederah, and Gederothaim. Fourteen towns with their villages.
15.41: Gederoth, Beth-dagon, Naamah, and Makkedah. Sixteen towns with their villages.
15.44: Keilah, Achzib, and Mareshah. Nine towns with their villages.
15.45: Ekron with its towns and villages;
15.46: from Ekron to the coast — all the territory near Ashdod — with their villages;
15.47: Ashdod with its towns and villages, Gaza with its towns and villages, reaching the Wadi of Egypt and the Mediterranean as its border.
15.48: In the highlands: Shamir, Jattir, Socoh,
15.51: Goshen, Holon, and Giloh. Eleven towns with their villages.
15.54: Humtah, Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron), and Zior. Nine towns with their villages.
15.57: Kain, Gibeah, and Timnah. Ten towns with their villages.
15.59: Maarath, Beth-anoth, and Eltekon. Six towns with their villages.
15.60: Kiriath-baal (that is, Kiriath-jearim) and Rabbah. Two towns with their villages.
15.61: In the desert wilderness: Beth-arabah, Middin, Secacah,
15.62: Nibshan, the City of Salt, and En-gedi. Six towns with their villages.
15.63: The Jebusites who lived in Jerusalem, however, Judah could not expel. The Jebusites have lived there among Judah's people from that day to this — a failure that will have long consequences.

16.1: The allotment for the house of Joseph started at the Jordan near Jericho — at the springs east of Jericho — and ran through the desert up into the highlands toward Bethel.
16.2: From Bethel the line passed through Luz and ran along to Ataroth, on the Archite frontier.
16.3: The boundary descended westward through Japhletite territory to Lower Beth-horon and on to Gezer, where it ended at the sea.
16.4: In this way Joseph's two sons — Manasseh and Ephraim — received their inheritance.
16.5: The boundary of Ephraim by their clans: on the eastern side it ran from Ataroth-addar to Upper Beth-horon.
16.6: On the north the border ran to the sea at Michmethath, then curved eastward to Taanath-shiloh and along to Janoah.
16.7: From Janoah it descended to Ataroth and Naarah, skirted Jericho, and met the Jordan.
16.8: The border ran from Tappuah westward along the Kanah Ravine to the sea. This was Ephraim's inheritance by their clans.
16.9: There were also towns reserved for Ephraim within Manasseh's territory, along with all their surrounding villages.
16.10: However, Ephraim never expelled the Canaanites living in Gezer. They remain among Ephraim's people to this day — pressed into labor, but not driven out. The pattern of incomplete obedience continues.

17.1: The allotment for Manasseh — the firstborn of Joseph: Machir, Manasseh's firstborn and father of Gilead, was granted Gilead and Bashan because he was a warrior.
17.2: The remaining clans of Manasseh also received portions — the clans of Abiezer, Helek, Asriel, Shechem, Hepher, and Shemida: the male descendants of Manasseh son of Joseph.
17.3: Zelophehad son of Hepher — son of Gilead, son of Machir, son of Manasseh — had no sons, only daughters: Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.
17.4: They appeared before Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the assembly's leaders and said: 'The LORD commanded Moses to give us an inheritance among our relatives.' Following the LORD's command, Joshua gave them an inheritance among their father's brothers.
17.5: So Manasseh received ten portions west of the Jordan — in addition to Gilead and Bashan on the east —
17.6: because Manasseh's daughters received their portion alongside his sons. The remaining sons held the land of Gilead.
17.7: Manasseh's border ran from Asher to Michmethath east of Shechem, then turned southward toward the settlement of En-tappuah.
17.8: The lands around Tappuah were Manasseh's, but the town of Tappuah itself — right on the border — belonged to Ephraim.
17.9: The border went down along the Kanah Ravine — on the south bank. The Ephraimite towns here were set within Manasseh's territory; Manasseh's northern line ran along the ravine to the sea.
17.10: South of the ravine was Ephraim; north was Manasseh. The sea formed the western edge. Asher lay to the north and Issachar to the east.
17.11: Manasseh also held enclaves within Issachar and Asher: Beth-shean with its surrounding towns, Ibleam with its towns, Dor with its towns, En-dor with its towns, Taanach with its towns, and Megiddo with its towns — those three highland regions.
17.12: But the Manassites could not expel the Canaanites from those towns — the Canaanites held their ground in that land.
17.13: When Israel's strength grew, they put the Canaanites to forced labor — but they never fully drove them out. Tribute is not obedience; this half-measure will bear bitter fruit.
17.14: The descendants of Joseph confronted Joshua: 'Why have you given us only a single lot? We are a great people — the LORD has blessed us abundantly. One portion is not enough!'
17.15: 'If you are such a great people,' Joshua answered, 'go up into the forest and clear it for yourselves in the Perizzite and Rephaim country. If the highlands of Ephraim can't contain you, make room for yourselves there.'
17.16: The Josephites pushed back: 'The highlands aren't enough. And the Canaanites in the valley have iron chariots — those in Beth-shean and its towns, those in the Valley of Jezreel. We cannot face them.'
17.17: Joshua stood firm with the house of Joseph — Ephraim and Manasseh both: 'You are a great people with great strength. You will not have just one portion.'
17.18: 'The forest highlands are yours — all of them. Clear them. The whole range will be yours to its farthest edge. And you will drive out the Canaanites, iron chariots and all. Their strength is no match for the one who gave you this land.'

18.1: The whole assembly of Israel gathered at Shiloh and erected the tent of meeting there. The land was subdued — this gathering at Shiloh marks a new center of worship, the ark finding its first permanent home in the promised land.
18.2: Seven tribes of Israel, however, still had not been given their inheritance.
18.3: Joshua challenged the people: 'How long will you drag your feet about going in to take hold of the land the LORD God of your ancestors has given you?'
18.4: Choose three men from each tribe. I will send them out to survey the land — to walk through it and write a description of it for distributing as inheritances. Bring their report back to me.
18.5: They are to divide it into seven portions. Judah keeps its territory in the south, Joseph's house keeps its territory in the north.
18.6: Write down the seven sections and bring the description to me here. I will cast the lots for you in the presence of the LORD our God.
18.7: Remember: the Levites get no land portion among you — the LORD's priesthood is their inheritance. And Gad, Reuben, and the half-tribe of Manasseh have already been settled east of the Jordan by Moses the LORD's servant.
18.8: The men rose up and went. Joshua gave them their charge: 'Travel through the land, write up a full description, and return to me — I will cast the lots for you here before the LORD at Shiloh.'
18.9: The men traveled through the land, recorded all the towns in seven sections in a written document, and brought it back to Joshua at the Shiloh camp.
18.10: Joshua cast the lots before the LORD at Shiloh, and there he distributed the land to each of the remaining tribes — the LORD's own allocation, made known through the lot.
18.11: The lot for the tribe of Benjamin fell to their clans. Their territory lay between Judah and Joseph — held between the two great houses.
18.12: Benjamin's northern border started at the Jordan, climbed the north slope of Jericho, went up westward through the highlands, and came out at the wilderness of Beth-aven.
18.13: From there it ran past Luz — the south side of Luz, which is Bethel — then descended to Ataroth-addar near the hill south of Lower Beth-horon.
18.14: The border curved westward, going south from the hill south of Beth-horon, and ended at Kiriath-baal — that is, Kiriath-jearim, a Judahite city. That was the western side.
18.15: The southern boundary began at the edge of Kiriath-jearim. From there it ran west to the spring at Nephtoah.
18.16: The border descended to the far end of the hill above the Valley of Ben-hinnom, at the north tip of the Valley of Rephaim. From there it dropped into the Hinnom Valley along the south slope of Jebusite Jerusalem and down to En-rogel.
18.17: From En-rogel the line bent north to En-shemesh, passed toward Geliloth opposite the Ascent of Adummim, and dropped to the Stone of Bohan son of Reuben.
18.18: The border then ran north along the slope above the Arabah valley and descended into it.
18.19: The line ran along the north side of Beth-hoglah and came to an end at the north bay of the Dead Sea, where the Jordan empties in. That was the southern boundary.
18.20: The Jordan served as the eastern border. This was Benjamin's inheritance — its full boundaries on every side — allotted to their clans.
18.21: Benjamin's towns by their clans included: Jericho, Beth-hoglah, the Valley of Keziz,
18.24: Chephar-ammoni, Ophni, and Geba. Twelve towns with their villages.
18.28: Zelah, Haeleph, Jebus — that is, Jerusalem — Gibeah, and Kiriath. Fourteen towns with their villages. This was Benjamin's inheritance by their clans.

19.1: The second lot fell to Simeon. The Simeonites received their allotment nested inside Judah's territory — already hinting at the fragmentation Jacob had foreseen when he cursed the violence of Simeon and Levi.
19.2: Their towns were: Beersheba, Sheba, and Moladah —
19.6: Beth-lebaoth and Sharuhen — thirteen cities in all, with their surrounding villages.
19.7: and also Ain, Rimmon, Ether, and Ashan — four more towns with their villages.
19.8: together with all the settlements around these towns stretching to Baalath-beer, also called Ramah of the Negeb. That is the full inheritance of the tribe of Simeon, assigned according to their clans.
19.9: Simeon's share was carved out of Judah's — Judah's allotment had been too large for them to fill. So Simeon settled within Judah, a tribe without its own borders, just as Jacob had foretold.
19.10: The third lot fell to Zebulun. Their boundary extended as far as Sarid.
19.11: Going westward, the line climbed to Maralah, touched Dabbesheth, and ran on to the ravine east of Jokneam.
19.12: From Sarid the line swung east toward the sunrise — to Chisloth-tabor, then to Daberath, and up to Japhia.
19.13: Eastward still, the boundary reached Gath-hepher and Eth-kazin, and ended at Rimmon on its way around toward Neah.
19.14: On the north the line curved around to Hannathon and terminated at the Valley of Iphtahel.
19.15: Within this boundary: Kattath, Nahalal, Shimron, Idalah, and Bethlehem — twelve towns in all, each with its surrounding villages.
19.16: That is Zebulun's inheritance, assigned by clan — those towns and their outlying settlements.
19.17: The fourth lot fell to Issachar, allotted to the Issacharites according to their clans.
19.18: Their towns included Jezreel, Chesulloth, and Shunem —
19.22: The boundary ran to Tabor, Shahazimah, and Beth-shemesh and ended at the Jordan — sixteen towns in all, with their villages.
19.23: That is Issachar's inheritance, assigned by clan — those cities and their outlying villages.
19.24: The fifth lot fell to Asher, distributed among their clans.
19.25: Their towns included Helkath, Hali, Beten, and Achshaph —
19.26: Alammelech, Amad, and Mishal — with the boundary reaching westward to Carmel and to Shihor-libnath.
19.27: Turning east, the line went to Beth-dagon, touched Zebulun and the Valley of Iphtahel on the north, continued to Beth-emek and Neiel, and reached Cabul on the north.
19.28: and on to Ebron, Rehob, Hammon, and Kanah — all the way up to Greater Sidon on the Phoenician coast.
19.29: The boundary turned to Ramah, touched the fortress city of Tyre, curved to Hosah, and reached the Mediterranean at Achzib on the coast —
19.30: with Ummah, Aphek, and Rehob included — twenty-two towns with their surrounding settlements.
19.31: That is Asher's inheritance, assigned by clan — those cities and their villages.
19.32: The sixth lot fell to Naphtali, distributed among their clans.
19.33: Their boundary started at Heleph — at the great oak of Zaanannim — ran through Adami-nekeb and Jabneel to Lakkum, and ended at the Jordan.
19.34: Turning west, the line went to Aznoth-tabor and out to Hukkok — bordering Zebulun to the south, Asher to the west, and Judah along the Jordan to the east.
19.35: Naphtali's fortified towns were: Ziddim, Zer, Hammath, Rakkath, and Chinnereth —
19.38: Iron, Migdal-el, Horem, Beth-anath, and Beth-shemesh — nineteen cities in all, with their villages.
19.39: That is Naphtali's inheritance, assigned by clan — those towns and their surrounding villages.
19.40: The seventh and final lot fell to Dan, distributed among their clans.
19.41: Their assigned towns included Zorah, Eshtaol, and Ir-shemesh —
19.46: and Me-jarkon and Rakkon, with the coastal area across from Joppa.
19.47: Dan could not hold the territory they had been given — the Amorites pressed them back into the hills. So they went north, attacked the city of Leshem, and conquered it by the sword. They settled there and renamed it Dan after their ancestor — but the original allotment remained beyond their reach, a sign of the troubles ahead in Judges.
19.48: That is Dan's inheritance, assigned by clan — those towns with their surrounding villages.
19.49: When all the tribal allotments had been settled boundary by boundary, the Israelites as a whole gave Joshua son of Nun his own share among them — last of all, after every tribe had received its portion.
19.50: Following the LORD's direction, they gave Joshua the city he had asked for — Timnath-serah in Ephraim's hill country. He rebuilt it from the ground up and made it his home: the leader who had received no land until last now settled in the highlands he had first scouted forty-five years before.
19.51: These are all the allotments that Eleazar the priest and Joshua son of Nun and the tribal heads assigned by lot before the LORD at Shiloh, at the entrance to the tent of meeting. The land had been divided. The promise, from Genesis onward, had reached its geographical fulfilment.

20.2: "Say to the Israelites: 'Establish the cities of refuge I ordained through Moses —'
20.3: 'so that anyone who takes a life by accident — unintentionally, without forethought or hatred — can flee to safety from the avenger of blood. The cities are Israel's provision for mercy in a world where blood cries out for blood.'
20.4: 'He is to present himself at the gate and state his case before the city elders. They must bring him inside, assign him lodging, and receive him as their own — the community standing between him and lethal vengeance while his case awaits full judgment.'
20.5: 'If the blood avenger comes in pursuit, the city must not surrender the fugitive — for he acted without premeditation, without hatred. The city's protection is the difference between justice and mob vengeance.'
20.6: 'He stays there — protected — until the congregation adjudicates his case, and until the sitting high priest dies. The high priest's death functions as a kind of corporate atonement that releases the fugitive. Only then may he go home. This is how Israel keeps justice and mercy together.'
20.7: They set apart three cities west of the Jordan: Kedesh in Galilee among Naphtali's hills, Shechem in Ephraim's hill country, and Kiriath-arba — ancient Hebron — in the Judahite highlands.
20.8: East of the Jordan — three more cities to mirror the three in the west: Bezer on the Reubenite plateau, Ramoth in Gilead for Gad, and Golan in Bashan for Manasseh. Six cities in all, accessible to every Israelite no matter where they lived.
20.9: These six cities were open to every Israelite and to every foreigner living among them. Accidental death need not trigger an endless cycle of reprisal. Israel was given institutions of mercy — a mark that the LORD's justice was not raw vengeance but ordered, restorable life.

21.1: Now the Levitical clan heads came before Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the tribal leaders of Israel.
21.2: Speaking at Shiloh in Canaan, they said: 'The LORD charged Moses that we Levites be given towns to settle in, with open land around them for our flocks and herds.'
21.3: At the LORD's command, the Israelites gave the Levites towns and surrounding pasturelands from their own tribal portions.
21.4: The first lot fell to the Kohathites. Aaron's priestly descendants received thirteen cities by lot from the tribes of Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin — settled in the south, near Jerusalem, where the priestly duties would one day be centred.
21.5: The other Kohathite families received ten cities by lot from Ephraim, Dan, and the western half of Manasseh.
21.6: The Gershonites received thirteen cities by lot from Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and the eastern half of Manasseh in Bashan.
21.7: The Merarites received twelve cities from Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun.
21.8: The whole allocation was carried out by lot, exactly as the LORD had instructed through Moses. The Levites, who had received no tribal territory, were given homes distributed throughout the land — permanently embedded among all Israel.
21.9: From Judah and Simeon came the following cities, listed here by name:
21.10: Aaron's descendants — Kohathite Levites — drew the first lot and were given:
21.11: Kiriath-arba — Hebron, city of the ancient giant Arba — in Judah's hill country, with open pastureland all around it.
21.12: The surrounding farmlands and outlying villages, however, belonged to Caleb son of Jephunneh as his personal inheritance — the city and the countryside divided between priest and warrior.
21.13: To Aaron's priestly line: Hebron — a city of refuge for those who kill unintentionally — with open pastureland, and Libnah with its pastureland,
21.14: Jattir with its pastureland, Eshtemoa with its pastureland,
21.15: Holon with its pastureland, Debir with its pastureland,
21.16: Ain with its pastureland, Juttah with its pastureland, and Beth-shemesh with its pastureland — nine cities drawn from Judah and Simeon.
21.17: From Benjamin: Gibeon with its pastureland, Geba with its pastureland,
21.18: Anathoth with its pastureland, and Almon with its pastureland — four cities.
21.19: In total, the Aaronic priests received thirteen cities with open pastureland — housed throughout the south, close to the heartland of Israel's future worship.
21.20: The remaining Kohathite families — those not of Aaron's priestly line — received their cities from Ephraim:
21.21: Shechem — another city of refuge for the unintentional killer — in Ephraim's hill country, with pastureland, and Gezer with its pastureland,
21.22: Kibzaim with its pastureland, and Beth-horon with its pastureland — four cities.
21.23: From Dan: Eltekeh with its pastureland, Gibbethon with its pastureland,
21.24: Aijalon with its pastureland, Gath-rimmon with its pastureland — four cities.
21.25: From the western half of Manasseh: Taanach with its pastureland, and Gath-rimmon with its pastureland — two cities.
21.26: The non-Aaronic Kohathites received ten cities in total with their pastureland.
21.27: To the Gershonites, from the eastern half of Manasseh: Golan in Bashan — a city of refuge — with its pastureland, and Beeshterah with its pastureland — two cities.
21.28: From Issachar: Kishon with its pastureland, Daberath with its pastureland,
21.29: Jarmuth with its pastureland, En-gannim with its pastureland — four cities.
21.30: From Asher: Mishal with its pastureland, Abdon with its pastureland,
21.31: Helkath with its pastureland, and Rehob with its pastureland — four cities.
21.32: From Naphtali: Kedesh in Galilee — another city of refuge for the accidental killer — with its pastureland, Hammoth-dor with its pastureland, and Kartan with its pastureland — three cities.
21.33: The Gershonites received thirteen cities in total with their pastureland.
21.34: To the Merarites — the last of the Levitical families — from Zebulun: Jokneam with its pastureland, and Kartah with its pastureland,
21.35: Dimnah with its pastureland, and Nahalal with its pastureland — four cities.
21.36: From Reuben: Bezer with its pastureland, Jahaz with its pastureland,
21.37: Kedemoth with its pastureland, and Mephaath with its pastureland — four cities.
21.38: From Gad: Ramoth in Gilead — a city of refuge — with its pastureland, and Mahanaim with its pastureland,
21.39: Heshbon with its pastureland, and Jazer with its pastureland — four cities.
21.40: The Merarites received twelve cities in all — the last allocation, completing the Levitical distribution.
21.41: In all, forty-eight cities with their surrounding pasturelands were set aside for the Levites within Israel's territories — one tribe distributed among all the others, as the LORD had ordained through Moses.
21.42: Every single one of these forty-eight cities had open common land around it for livestock — the same provision for each. The Levites had no tribal territory, but they had a home everywhere.
21.43: The LORD gave Israel every part of the land he had sworn to their ancestors. They took possession of it and lived in it.
21.44: The LORD gave them rest — unbroken, complete — on every side. Not one enemy stood firm before them. Every foe fell into Israel's hand, because the LORD fought for them. What he swore, he kept.
21.45: Not one word. Not one promise. Every good thing the LORD had spoken to Israel came to pass without exception. The land, the rest, the victory, the Levitical cities, the cities of refuge — all of it. Genesis 12 had promised a land, a people, a blessing. Joshua 21 is the answer.

22.1: Joshua summoned the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh —
22.2: and addressed them: "You have kept every command Moses the LORD's servant laid on you. Every order I gave, you obeyed.
22.3: You have not abandoned your brothers — not in all these years of fighting — but held to the LORD's charge without fail.
22.4: Now the LORD your God has settled your brothers in the rest he promised them. The war is over. Go home — back to your tents, to the land east of the Jordan that Moses gave you.
22.5: Only — take this with you: be diligent, deeply careful, to live by the commandment and Torah Moses gave you. Love the LORD your God. Walk in all his ways. Keep his commands. Hold fast to him. Serve him with your whole heart and your whole self. This is the Shema lived out. Do not let distance make you forget."
22.6: Joshua gave them his blessing and released them, and they set out for home.
22.7: Half of Manasseh had received their land in Bashan from Moses; the other half Joshua had settled west of the Jordan among their brothers. As Joshua dismissed the eastern half and blessed them,
22.8: he told them: "Take home great wealth — abundant flocks, silver and gold, bronze and iron, clothing in abundance. Divide the enemy's spoil generously with the brothers you fought alongside."
22.9: The Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh left their brothers at Shiloh in Canaan and headed for Gilead — the land they had earned by their service, granted them as the LORD had commanded through Moses.
22.10: When they reached the Jordan on the Canaan side, before crossing over, the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh built a large, prominent altar by the river — conspicuous, impossible to miss.
22.11: Word reached the rest of Israel: "The Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh have built an altar at the border of Canaan — right at the Jordan, on our side of the river."
22.12: The response was swift and alarmed. The whole Israelite community assembled at Shiloh, ready to march to war. An altar built outside the sanctioned worship site looked like a second center of religion — apostasy in stone.
22.13: But before attacking, Israel sent an embassy to Gilead — Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest leading the delegation —
22.14: with ten senior leaders, one from every tribe — men of real authority, representing all Israel.
22.15: They arrived in Gilead and laid the charge before the eastern tribes:
22.16: "The entire assembly of the LORD sends this charge: What is this betrayal against Israel's God? You have turned away from the LORD by building yourselves an altar — as if in defiance of him today.
22.17: Have we already forgotten Peor? That sin clung to us; the plague it brought has barely faded from memory. Are you bringing another Peor upon us?
22.18: And now you are turning away from the LORD! If you rebel today, his anger will fall on all of Israel tomorrow — not just you. That is how it works: one community's sin, everyone's judgment.
22.19: If you feel cut off — if the east bank feels spiritually outside the LORD's presence — then come over. Cross the Jordan and settle among us in the land of the LORD's tabernacle. We will make room for you. But do not erect a rival altar. There is only one place, one worship, one people before the LORD.
22.20: Remember Achan son of Zerah. His private act of treachery in the matter of the devoted goods brought the LORD's wrath down on the whole congregation. He was not alone in dying for it — thirty-six men fell at Ai. One man's sin; all Israel bleeds. This altar carries the same risk."
22.21: The eastern tribes answered the Israelite leaders directly and solemnly:
22.22: "El, God, the LORD — El, God, the LORD! He himself is our witness. The triple name is our oath. Let all Israel hear: if we built this altar in rebellion or in treachery against the LORD, then do not spare us.
22.23: If we built this altar to worship elsewhere — to offer sacrifices apart from the tabernacle — let the LORD deal with us. We invoke him as our judge.
